entities.name: śabda-bhāvanā
entities.cite: a conceiving or intending in word

entities.name: karaṇa-vinyāsa
entities.cite: the initial karana-vinyasa (tongue and lips taking position)

entities.name: sthāna
entities.cite: the striking upon the places of articulation

entities.name: ajasra-vṛtti
entities.cite: the unceasing inner operation that no act initiates because it never began

entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: a wording that is always already under way

entities.name: vāk
entities.cite: audible speaking is a local intensification (the fan of 1.116) of a wording that is always already under way

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: Each of these, the verse notes, is itself a piece of itikartavyata — a procedure — and so by 1.121 requires sabda-bhavana, a conceiving or intending in word, to occur at all.

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: Each of these, the verse notes, is itself a piece of itikartavyata — a procedure — and so by 1.121 requires sabda-bhavana, a conceiving or intending in word, to occur at all.

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: Each of these, the verse notes, is itself a piece of itikartavyata — a procedure — and so by 1.121 requires sabda-bhavana, a conceiving or intending in word, to occur at all.

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: it terminates, non-viciously, in the ajasra-vrtti word of 1.116 — the unceasing inner operation that no act initiates because it never began.

axioms.name: vivartate
axioms.cite: audible speaking is a local intensification (the fan of 1.116) of a wording that is always already under way.

denials.name: originates_from_silence
denials.cite: Speech does not start from silence; audible speaking is a local intensification (the fan of 1.116) of a wording that is always already under way.

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: none occurs without a conceiving in word

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: none occurs without a conceiving in word

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: none occurs without a conceiving in word

rejected_readings.label: silent_private_act
rejected_readings.rendering: Conceiving in word is a purely private mental act isolated from the motor acts of speech production.
rejected_readings.why: Misconstrues sabda-bhavana as an autonomous inner state divorced from the utterance machinery it grounds. The axioms establish that word-conceiving is the asraya (ground) of all three motor acts; treating it as independent breaks the unified model of speech the commentary enforces.

rejected_readings.label: origin_from_silence
rejected_readings.rendering: Speech originates from an initial silence and then becomes activated into word-forming.
rejected_readings.why: Asserts that utterance emerges from a pre-linguistic void, contradicting the cardinal denial: 'Speech does not start from silence.' The commentary insists audible speaking is a 'local intensification' of a wording 'always already under way', grounded eternally in ajasra-vrtti.

rejected_readings.label: sequential_motor_autonomy
rejected_readings.rendering: The motor acts of speech (positioning, breathing, striking) happen independently and then sabda-bhavana coordinates them afterward.
rejected_readings.why: Reverses the dependence: makes motor acts autonomous and word-conceiving their post-hoc orchestrator. The axioms invert this—sabda-bhavana is the asraya (ground/support) of the motor acts, not their consequence. Motor acts occur *only* because they are grounded in word-conceiving.

notes: The verse articulates the paradox of speech: even physical motor acts (positioning, breathing, striking) require inner word-conceiving (sabda-bhavana), which is itself grounded in the unceasing subtle wording (ajasra-vrtti of 1.116). The regress is non-vicious because it terminates in an operation that no act initiates. Audible utterance is a local intensification of an inner wording already always under way. This welds physiology (1.108-115) to epistemology: even the body's speech machinery runs on the very medium it outputs.
